title: The Smith's Quench Log — recovering a hidden two-regime cooling law
strategies: trivial: predict a flat constant delta (the mean of the logged dT column) -> reproduces the checker's own baseline exactly, Ratio ~= 0.1

greedy: fit a single-mechanism affine model dT = p*T + q by ordinary least squares on the logged rows; explains the logged range almost perfectly (the cubic term barely perturbs any single point there) but omits the radiative regime entirely, so it collapses on the far-hotter held-out temperatures where that term dominates

strong: the insight -- don't trust the affine model's already-excellent training R^2 as proof there is nothing more to model (the cubic term is smaller than the reading noise on every single row, so no in-sample goodness-of-fit signal singles it out); commit instead to the physically-motivated two-term additive decomposition dT = a*(T-AMBIENT) + b*(T-AMBIENT)**3 and jointly fit BOTH coefficients by ordinary least squares over the entire log at once -- by Frisch-Waugh-Lovell this is exactly the correctly-partialled residual signal, and aggregating every row (not any single point) is what makes the small coefficient recoverable, so the law now extrapolates correctly

headroom above strong: robustify the coefficient recovery (e.g. iteratively re-weighted or trimmed least squares against the noisy log) to shave the remaining held-out error toward the irreducible noise floor
